body:
## Problem

`a-organvm` can be clean at the repo level while still losing session continuity if the local Claude/Codex memory backing this instance is not mirrored remotely. The current relay already identifies this as a vacuum: `~/.claude/projects/-Users-4jp-Workspace-a-organvm/` is not tracked by chezmoi, so machine loss would break continuity even if the repo itself survives.

## Required invariant

`[(local):(remote) = 1:1]`

If the physical manifestation dies, the soul must persist.

## Why this matters

This is not ordinary backup hygiene. In this organism, persistent memory is part of the operating substrate. A clean git tree is not enough if the working memory layer is single-homed.

## Deliverable

Implement and document a durable mirroring path for instance memory so that:

- local session memory has a remote counterpart
- recovery procedure is explicit and testable
- session-close protocol can verify mirror health before declaring the organism safe to close

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] The `a-organvm` session-memory location is identified canonically
- [ ] A remote mirror path is established and documented
- [ ] Recovery steps are written and rehearsable
- [ ] Close-safety check distinguishes repo cleanliness from continuity safety
- [ ] Relay / operator docs reference the mirror requirement

## Source

- `a-organvm/RELAY.md` critical maintenance note
- `a-organvm/.codex/plans/2026-04-02-temporal-maturity-vacuum-audit.md`

## Notes

This issue is about continuity infrastructure, not ordinary code backup. It closes the current vacuum where source can persist while session memory dies.
title: OPS-001 session memory mirroring: enforce 1:1 local and remote continuity

## Failure state
The source file `INST-INDEX-RERUM-FACIENDARUM.md` contains a newly added open item:

- `IRF-SYS-044`

But `organvm irf stats` still reports the old totals immediately afterward:

- `Total: 715`
- `Open: 384`
- `SYS: 29`

Expected from file contents after insertion:

- `Total: 716`
- `Open: 385`
- `SYS: 30`

## Why this matters
The IRF CLI is being used as a governance truth source during session closeout. If it silently undercounts new items, operators can incorrectly conclude the registry is synchronized when it is not.

## Reproduction
1. Add a new `IRF-SYS-*` row near the tail of `INST-INDEX-RERUM-FACIENDARUM.md`.
2. Run `organvm irf stats`.
3. Compare CLI totals to the file contents.

## Observed on
- Date: 2026-04-02
- Repo: `meta-organvm/organvm-corpvs-testamentvm`
- During closeout of `public-record-data-scrapper` reliability hardening session

## Suggested investigation
- Check whether the IRF parser stops at a sentinel, duplicate header, or statistics block boundary.
- Add a parser test with a tail-appended item near the final open section.
- Fail loudly if the source file and derived counts disagree during refresh tooling.
title: fix(irf): stats command undercounts newly added tail items

body:
## Failure state
In `organvm-iii-ergon/public-record-data-scrapper`, both required session-close commands hang with no output and require an external timeout:

- `organvm session review --latest`
- `organvm session plans --project .`

Observed on 2026-04-02 from repo root. Both exit `124` when wrapped with `timeout 20`, producing no stdout/stderr before termination.

## Why this matters
These commands are mandated by repo session-close protocol in `CLAUDE.md`. A silent hang means session closeout cannot be completed deterministically, which creates governance and memory-loss risk.

## Reproduction
```bash
cd ~/Workspace/organvm-iii-ergon/public-record-data-scrapper
timeout 20 organvm session review --latest
timeout 20 organvm session plans --project .
```

## Expected
Commands should either complete or fail with actionable diagnostics.

## Actual
Both commands block until killed by timeout.

## Related context
- `organvm prompts distill --dry-run` is not hanging, but fails fast because `data/atoms/clipboard-prompts.json` is missing and tells the operator to run `organvm prompts clipboard` first.
- This was discovered during closeout of IRF-APP-003 work for `public-record-data-scrapper`.

## Suggested investigation
- Reproduce under profiler / debug logging.
- Determine whether the stall is transcript discovery, plan indexing, git inspection, or corpus path resolution.
- Add timeout / progress diagnostics so the failure mode is visible even before root cause is fixed.
title: fix(session): investigate hanging review and plans commands in large repos

body:
## Scope
Phase 1 reliability hardening for the Render-hosted API service (`ucc-mca-api`).

## Failure state mapped
- Production startup was using `tsx` against `server/index.ts`, which can spike memory during initialization on Render.
- Telemetry persistence expected `available_strategies`, but the schema path was drifted/missing.
- Startup telemetry hydration loaded per-state history without an explicit bound or kill switch.

## Implemented in current working tree
- Switch production `start` to `node dist/server.cjs`.
- Add schema support for `available_strategies` in base migration/schema.
- Add forward repair migration for existing databases.
- Bound startup telemetry hydration and add env controls to skip or cap it.

## Remaining to close the loop
- Deploy the latest build to Render with `npm run build:render`.
- Apply DB migration `013_ingestion_telemetry_available_strategies.sql` in the target environment.
- Verify the API boots cleanly on Render after deployment.
- Decide whether the worker runtime also needs a bundled production entrypoint for parity.

## References
- IRF: `IRF-APP-003`
- Render service: `srv-d6hh48fkijhs73fgk00g`
title: IRF-APP-003: Phase 1 reliability hardening for ucc-mca-api

body:
## Context

System-wide process recording infrastructure built in Phases 1-4 (commit `add3902`). Records semantic actions as ideas move through parameter space, composes them into sequences and chains, detects repeated cycles across sessions.

Conceptual prior art: Alchemical Synthesizer (ORGAN-II) — module registry, patch bay, CHRONOS automation.

## What Exists (Phases 1-4)

- `action_ledger/schemas.py` — Action, Sequence, Chain, Route, ParamAxis, ParamRegistry (open parameter model)
- `action_ledger/ledger.py` — atomic record(), compose_chain(), close_session(), YAML persistence
- `action_ledger/routes.py` — bidirectional route graph, lineage tracing, provenance injection
- `action_ledger/cycles.py` — 4 detection methods: verb sequences, trajectories, intents, stalls
- `action_ledger/cli.py` — record, show, sequence, chain, routes, cycles, params
- 57 tests, 221 total suite

## Remaining Work

### Phase 5 — Integration (IRF-OSS-032)
Wire contrib_engine modules to emit actions into the ledger:
- [ ] `fieldwork.py` calls `ledger.record()` on observation capture
- [ ] `campaign.py` calls `ledger.record()` on phase transitions
- [ ] `monitor.py` calls `ledger.record()` on PR state changes

### Convergence (IRF-OSS-033)
The action ledger and the dispatch ledger (`tool-interaction-design/conductor/`) are the same system at different scales, built independently on consecutive days. Must converge:
- [ ] Dispatch receipt → action type
- [ ] Timecard → sequence start/close
- [ ] Energy ledger → parameter axes
- [ ] Scorecard → cycle detection scoped to agent performance

### Design Spec (IRF-OSS-034)
- [ ] Write formal spec to `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-31-action-ledger-design.md`

## IRF Cross-References
IRF-OSS-032, IRF-OSS-033, IRF-OSS-034

## Plan
`.claude/plans/2026-03-31-action-ledger-plan.md`
title: Action Ledger: Phase 5 integration + convergence with dispatch ledger

## Summary
Full implementation plan exists in `specs/layer-1-dialogue-loop-tracker/tasks.md` with 60 tasks across 9 phases.

## Phases
1. **Setup** (T001-T004): Infrastructure scaffolding — PARTIALLY DONE
2. **Foundational** (T005-T009): Prisma migrations, speaker parser, API structure, frontend client
3. **Core CRUD** (T010-T018): Scene/Speaker/Phrase endpoints + UI
4. **Loop & Sequence** (T019-T025): Repeat count, stagger, loop mode, duration mode, start triggers
5. **TTS Integration** (T026-T032): Provider abstraction, ElevenLabs, Coqui, VALL-E, Redis caching
6. **Playback Engine** (T033-T040): Full sequence audio, WebSocket, transport controls, visual tracker
7. **Versioning** (T041-T048): Save/restore/compare versions, text diff
8. **Voice & Speech** (T049-T053): Voice cloning, per-speaker rate/pitch controls
9. **Polish** (T054-T060): Performance optimization, error handling, edge cases

## Current State
Scaffold exists. Zero application logic implemented. Start at Phase 2 (T005).

## Summary

`discover_chatgpt_projects()` calls `backend-api/gizmos/discovery/mine` which is the "My GPTs" API, not the ChatGPT "Projects" (folder/organization) feature. The function returns 0 projects because the user has no custom GPTs, not because they have no Projects.

ChatGPT Projects (the folder feature for organizing conversations) use an undiscovered API endpoint. Need to reverse-engineer the correct endpoint from browser network traffic.

## Blocked by

IRF-CCE-026 (need a working session with full scope first to observe the Projects API)

## Fix

1. Open ChatGPT in Chrome with DevTools Network tab
2. Navigate to Projects UI
3. Capture the actual API endpoint
4. Update `discover_chatgpt_projects()` in `chatgpt_local_session.py`
title: IRF-CCE-027: Post Office discover uses wrong API (gizmos != Projects)

Discovered in S39 (2026-03-30). Previous S38 session (2026-03-26) saw full 633 conversations.

- Full ChatGPT corpus ingest (4/633 fetched)
- 60 ChatGPT project extractions (0 projects discoverable)
- DSP-alternative extraction completion
- IRF-CCE-027 (discover endpoint fix needs working session first)

## Fix

Either:
1. Re-launch ChatGPT desktop app and sign in fresh (may restore scope)
2. Use the official ChatGPT data export (requested in S38, undelivered)
3. Check `source-drop/chatgpt/inbox/` at each session start

body:
## Which agent handles which work type

### Routing table
| Work Type | Agent | Why |
|-----------|-------|-----|
| Isotope dissolution (mechanical) | Codex/OpenCode | Pattern-following, no judgment |
| Long-context cross-reference | Gemini | 1M+ context window |
| Architecture decisions | Claude | Needs SEED philosophy |
| Test execution (parallel) | Codex | Parallel, report results |
| SOP/doc triage | Gemini | Long-context reading |
| Signal-graph.yaml design | Claude | Architectural |
| Gate status updates | Any | Read YAML, update field |

### File
`a-organvm/AGENTS.md` — read by every agent at session start. Declares:
- What this agent CAN do in this instance
- What this agent CANNOT touch
- Where to find the relay from the last session
- The active gate contracts and their states

### Depends on
- NRV-005 relay handoff format defined
title: GEN-004 AGENTS.md: declare agent routing for cocoon work

body:
## Every session close must produce a relay prompt for the next agent

### Format (from conductor/handoff.py)
```yaml
relay:
  session: S43
  agent: claude-opus-4-6
  completed:
    - SEED.md v7 (1,124 lines)
    - 30 isotopes dissolved (968 tests)
    - 25 issues created with unique IDs
    - a-organvm instance created + pushed
  next:
    - GEN-002: Python packaging decision
    - SIG-001: signal-graph.yaml generator
    - SKL-001: skeletal--define test suite
  boundary:
    reads: [a-organvm/, meta-organvm/post-flood/, meta-organvm/organvm-engine/]
    writes: [a-organvm/]
    does_not_touch: [any source repo code — gates drive that work]
  containership:
    this_agent_owns: architecture decisions, SEED evolution, gate design
    next_agent_owns: isotope dissolution mechanics, test execution
    shared: signal-graph.yaml schema (design here, implement there)
```

### Rule
No session ends without a relay. The relay IS the handoff envelope. The next agent reads ONLY the relay + the SEED + the gate contracts. It does not read the previous session's full context. Fresh eyes.

### Depends on
- NRV-002 conductor handoff module
title: NRV-005 prompt relay handoffs: session boundary + containership declarations

body:
## Each signal type has a visual form

### Design
- FORMATION_ADDED → emergence glyph
- GATE_PASSED → opening glyph  
- ISOTOPE_DISSOLVED → absorption glyph
- STATE_CHANGED → transition glyph
- ATTRACTION_DETECTED → orbital glyph
- REDUNDANCY_DETECTED → collapse glyph

The glyph is GENERATED from the signal's properties (type, source mechanism, target mechanism, timestamp hash). Like autoglyphs: the data IS the art.

### Depends on
- SIG-004 emission protocol
- SIG-002 ledger populated
- ITG-001 integumentary cocoon (rendering surface)
title: SIG-005 autoglyph visual language: signal type → glyph mapping

body:
## When a function bolts, it emits — and the emission is recorded

### Protocol
1. Function added to signal-graph.yaml → EMIT: FORMATION_ADDED
2. Gate condition passes → EMIT: GATE_PASSED  
3. Isotope dissolved → EMIT: ISOTOPE_DISSOLVED
4. Cocoon state changes → EMIT: STATE_CHANGED
5. Signal attraction surfaces candidates → EMIT: ATTRACTION_DETECTED
6. Metabolic test fails → EMIT: REDUNDANCY_DETECTED

### Ledger format
Append-only JSONL (extends engine events/spine.py pattern):
```json
{"ts": "2026-03-30T22:00:00Z", "type": "ISOTOPE_DISSOLVED", "source": "alchemia/registry_loader", "target": "engine/registry/loader", "hash": "e800061"}
```

### Feeds
- SIG-002 (signal ledger storage)
- SIG-003 (autoglyph display)
- END-002 (seers interpret patterns)
title: SIG-004 signal emission protocol: every bolt records to ledger

body:
## Conductor dispatches agents to cocoons with isolation

### Architecture
conductor/fleet*.py already has:
- FleetRegistry (agent definitions from YAML)
- FleetRouter (score agents for task assignment)
- TaskDispatcher (classify work types, route to agents)
- CrossVerify (verify agent output against constraints)
- HandoffBrief (context packaging for agent-to-agent handoff)

### How it works for the molt
1. Each cocoon gate is a TASK with declared boundary (which repos, which files)
2. FleetRouter assigns agent (Claude/Gemini/Codex/OpenCode) based on work type
3. TaskDispatcher creates a scoped sandbox (MSC-004) per assignment
4. Agent works blind — cannot see other cocoons
5. CrossVerify checks output against gate conditions
6. HandoffBrief packages results for the next agent

### Depends on
- NRV-002 nervous--orchestrate converging
- MSC-004 isolation layer extracted
title: NRV-003 conductor fleet dispatch: agent routing for parallel cocoon work

body:
## Extract titan's isolation layer for cocoon work

Titan has 4 runtime backends (local/Docker/Firecracker/OpenFaaS) + 8 safety modules (gates, HITL, RBAC, sandbox, scope validator). Extract these as the isolation layer for molt agents.

### Architecture
Each dissolution/gate agent gets a scoped sandbox:
- Mounted: only repos relevant to that cocoon's gates (read-write)
- organvm-engine (read-only — canonical source)
- Cannot see: other cocoons, a-organvm contracts, memory

### Source
- agentic-titan/runtime/ (base, local, docker, sandbox, firecracker/, selector)
- agentic-titan/titan/safety/ (gates, hitl, rbac, filters, patterns, permissions, policies, sanitizer)
- agent--claude-smith/core/scope-validator.ts (ScopeValidator)

### Depends on
- MSC-002 titan cocoon converging
- GEN-002 Python packaging decision

### Boundary
This is EXTRACTION, not dependency. The organism uses titan's isolation patterns without depending on the full 94K-line framework.
title: MSC-004 titan runtime extraction: sandbox + scope isolation for molt agents

- AP-14: Celebration without interrogation

body:
## Decision

Creative Capital 2027 application **deferred** (S-CC-review, 2026-03-30). Not this cycle.

## Why

Funder-fit assessment determined CC funds **sensory art audiences encounter** (sculptures, games, installations, performances). ORGANVM currently produces **governance infrastructure**, not art. The Generative Testament (art-output layer) is V2 aspiration — not V1 reality.

## Re-evaluation trigger

When V2 generative layer produces art as byproduct of system operation.

## Preserved assets

All reusable: draft.md, bio.md, submission-checklist.md, work-sample-notes.md, scratch/rewrite-brief.md, winners analysis, handbook research.

## Process artifact

Funder-fit gate protocol established (replicable):
1. What does funder actually fund? (analyze winners, not mission)
2. What do we currently have? (inventory, not aspiration)
3. Alignment? (fit, not framing)
4. Go/no-go before drafting

## Cross-references

- IRF-INST-002 (P0→P3), IRF-APP-015 (P0→P3), DONE-284
- IRF-INST-019 (omega art gap), IRF-INST-020 (funder-fit SOP)

body:
**Owner:** Scott Lefler (Lefler Design)

## What
The engine works. We need real clients to pilot it. You have the relationships.

## Criteria for pilot clients
- Already paying Lefler Design for design/video work
- Producing premium hero content (K+ per asset)
- Struggling with social media consistency
- Would benefit from 30-90 days of auto-generated content
- Open to trying a new tool with hands-on support

## Action needed
1. List 3 existing clients who fit the criteria
2. For each: company name, primary contact, current content cadence, pain points
3. Rank by likelihood to say yes

## Deliverable
Short list (text/DM) with the 3 names and a sentence about each.
title: [scott.5] Identify first 3 pilot clients for content engine

body:
**Owner:** Scott Lefler (Lefler Design)

## What
The dashboard is functional but needs your design eye. You're the UI/UX partner — this is your domain.

## Review areas
1. **https://cronus-dashboard.pages.dev** — open on phone AND desktop
2. Color palette — is the grayscale + black too minimal?
3. Typography — sizing, weights, hierarchy
4. Card layouts — Review Queue cards, Asset cards, Dashboard stats
5. Navigation — sidebar (desktop) vs bottom nav (mobile)
6. Upload flow — does the toast notification + redirect feel right?
7. Settings page — is the provider configuration clear to a non-technical user?

Annotated screenshots or written notes with specific changes. Figma mockups if you want to redesign any section.

## Steps
1. Go to **https://cronus-dashboard.pages.dev**

- Are the captions on-brand for a design studio?

- What's missing from the generated posts?

body:
## Source

A **kindle** of kittens — the term of venery for the act of bringing forth new life. In agent systems: a topology pattern where agents spawn sub-agents that form their own independent formations.

## The Gap

Current topologies are flat — all agents exist in a single formation. There's no concept of an agent spawning a sub-group that operates in a different topology. The migration manager (hive/migration/) moves agents between runtimes but doesn't create new agent groups.

## The Pattern

A parent topology contains agents that can **kindle** — spawn child formations:
- A HIERARCHY agent delegates a sub-task by spawning a SWARM of workers
- A STAR hub creates a PIPELINE for a multi-step workflow
- A FISSION_FUSION cluster, during fusion, spawns a specialist sub-group for the crisis

The child formation has its own topology, metrics, and lifecycle. When the sub-task completes, the child formation dissolves and results flow back to the parent.

## Connection to Existing Architecture

- `hive/migration/manager.py` has `_spawn_in_target()` for cross-runtime spawning
- `titan/orchestration/` has the WorkflowEngine for DAG execution
- The AgentForge YAML DSL could specify nested topologies

## Biological Precedent

- Ant colonies spawn satellite colonies that operate independently
- Bee swarms split into scout groups that use different coordination patterns than the main hive
- Cell division: one cell becomes two, each potentially differentiating

Depends on: #57 (venery naming — "kindle" is the spawning topology's name)

labels.description: Exploratory research — no implementation path yet
labels.name: research

title: feat: spawning topology — agents that create sub-agents in new formations (a kindle)

body:
## Source

The Deus Ex Machina face in Matrix Revolutions is not decorative — it's the swarm's communication interface with Neo. The formation IS the function. The shape the swarm takes is how it presents its collective capability to the outside world.

## The Principle

An agent group's topology should be externally observable and meaningful. The topology IS the API surface:
- A STAR formation tells the caller: "talk to the hub, it will coordinate"
- A SWARM tells the caller: "broadcast to all, expect emergent response"
- A HIERARCHY tells the caller: "submit at the root, it delegates down"

Currently, topology is an internal implementation detail. External callers don't know or care what formation the agents are in. This issue proposes making topology a first-class part of the group's external interface.

## Implementation Ideas

- `TopologyDescriptor` that external callers can query: "what formation are you in, and what does that mean for how I should interact with you?"
- Formation-specific routing hints: the topology tells the caller the optimal message pattern
- Visual representation: topology state as a dashboard element (connects to MCP Apps pattern from dbt-mcp, see organvm-mcp-server #8)

## Also Relevant

- Big Hero 6 Microbots: neural headband (STAR) dissolves into linked formation (MESH) — the formation change IS the mode change
- Stargate Replicators: passive human form → active weapon form — topology serves different functions in different modes

Extends: #58 (programmable matter), #60 (continuous morphing)

labels.description: Exploratory research — no implementation path yet
labels.name: research

title: feat: formation-as-interface — topology IS the API surface

body:
## The Problem

The TopologyEngine currently does hard switches: SWARM → HIERARCHY → MESH. The agent group is either in one formation or another. This is like asking a flock of starlings to choose between "sphere" and "line" — in reality, murmurations flow continuously through shape-space, never settling into a named form.

## The Fix

Replace discrete topology selection with a **topology gradient** — a continuous blend between formation types, driven by local agent metrics.

### How It Would Work

Each agent maintains a local topology vector:
```python
topology_weights = {
    TopologyType.SWARM: 0.3,      # 30% all-to-all
    TopologyType.HIERARCHY: 0.5,   # 50% tree-like
    TopologyType.MESH: 0.2,        # 20% grid-like
}
```

The neighbor selection for message routing blends these weights:
- 50% of messages follow hierarchical paths
- 30% broadcast to swarm peers
- 20% route through mesh links

As task characteristics change, the weights shift continuously. No discrete "switch" event. The topology morphs like a murmuration changing shape.

### Connection to Existing Code

- `criticality.py` already measures order_parameter (0-1 system organization) — this IS the gradient signal
- `fission_fusion.py` has hysteresis for state transitions — extend to continuous blending
- `topology.py` BaseTopology's `get_message_targets()` can be modified to blend multiple routing strategies

### Biological Precedent

- Starling murmurations: shape emerges from local N=7 interactions, not from a central "pick a shape" command
- Fish schooling: school shape is a continuous function of predator proximity, food density, current
- Slime mold: *Physarum polycephalum* continuously morphs between exploration and exploitation modes

### Depends On

- #57 (venery naming — the named topologies become "attractor basins" in the continuous space rather than discrete states)
- #58 (programmable matter model — catom reconfiguration cost provides the gradient's energy function)

labels.description: Exploratory research — no implementation path yet
labels.name: research

title: feat: continuous topology morphing — gradient transitions between formations

body:
## Concept

Michael Crichton's *Prey* (2002) describes a nano-particle swarm that evolves predator-prey dynamics — the swarm hunts, learns from failures, and adapts its strategy across generations. The individual particles are simple; the intelligence is emergent.

### What This Means for agentic-titan

We already have the biological primitives:
- Stigmergy (ant pheromone trails)
- Fission-fusion (crow roost dynamics)
- Murmuration physics (N=7 topological neighbors)
- Criticality (edge-of-chaos sensitivity)

What's **missing** is the predator-prey dynamic itself — agents competing for resources, developing counter-strategies, and evolving through adversarial pressure.

### Three Implementation Layers

**1. Resource competition**: Agents compete for limited resources (LLM tokens, tool access, context window). Currently resources are allocated centrally. A Prey-style model would have agents forage for resources through the pheromone field — depositing RESOURCE traces when they find availability, WARNING traces when they encounter rate limits.

**2. Adversarial co-evolution**: In testing/red-teaming topologies, one group of agents tries to break something while another defends. The attacker swarm learns from failed attempts (reinforced WARNING traces), adapts strategy (topology reconfiguration), and the defender swarm counter-adapts. This is the Prey dynamic: intelligence emerges from adversarial iteration, not from initial programming.

**3. Generational learning**: Crichton's swarm evolved across generations — successful strategies propagated, failed ones died. Our `hive/learning.py` module exists but doesn't implement generational selection. Adding a fitness function that selects topology configurations based on task success rates would close this gap.

### Connection to Existing Architecture

| Prey Concept | Our Module | Gap |
|-------------|-----------|-----|
| Particle swarm | `topology.py` SwarmTopology | No resource foraging |
| Emergent behavior | `stigmergy.py` pheromone field | No adversarial traces |
| Adaptation | `fission_fusion.py` dynamic clustering | No generational selection |
| Self-replication | `migration/manager.py` spawn | No fitness-based reproduction |
| Predation | — | **Not implemented** |

### Why This Matters

The contribution engine's Absorption Protocol already operates on this principle — external questions are "predatory" probes that force the architecture to adapt. Formalizing this as a first-class dynamic in the agent runtime would make the system genuinely antifragile: adversarial pressure improves it.

labels.description: Exploratory research — no implementation path yet
labels.name: research

title: feat: Prey dynamics — emergent predator-prey behavior in agent swarms

body:
## Concept

Treat agent groups as **programmable matter** — a material that can dynamically change its physical properties (shape, density, moduli, conductivity) based on user input or autonomous sensing.

### The Analogy

In claytronics (Carnegie Mellon), individual units called **catoms** (claytronic atoms) can bond, unbond, and rearrange to form arbitrary 3D shapes. Each catom is simple; the shape emerges from collective reconfiguration.

Our agent topologies already do this partially — fission-fusion splits/merges clusters, the topology engine switches between patterns. But the transition is discrete (switch from one type to another). **Programmable matter implies continuous morphing** — the same set of agents smoothly reconfiguring their connection topology as the task shape changes.

### What This Means for agentic-titan

1. **Continuous topology morphing**: Instead of hard switches between SWARM → HIERARCHY → MESH, agents continuously adjust their neighbor connections based on local metrics. The topology is never in a named state — it's always in between, finding its shape.

2. **Shape-from-function**: The topology should emerge from the task's coordination requirements, not be selected a priori. If a task requires strong central coordination, a hub naturally forms. If it requires parallel exploration, the hub dissolves. The topology is the shadow of the task, not a pre-selected pattern.

3. **Reconfiguration cost model**: Catoms have an energy cost for bonding/unbonding. Agent reconfiguration has a cost too — context switching, state transfer, dropped work. The system should model this cost and minimize unnecessary reconfiguration.

### Inspiration Sources

- Claytronics (Goldstein, Mowry — CMU) — catom model
- Michael Crichton's *Prey* (2002) — nano-swarm that learns, adapts topology, and exhibits emergent predatory behavior from simple rules
- Ori Rosen's programmable matter work
- Our existing fission_fusion.py, criticality.py, and assembly.py already have the primitives

### Implementation Path

- `hive/matter.py` — ProgrammableMatterModel that holds the continuous topology state
- Modify TopologyEngine to support gradient transitions (not just type switches)
- Add reconfiguration cost metrics to the existing MetricsSink
- Wire into the existing criticality detection (phase transitions = reconfiguration events)

labels.description: Exploratory research — no implementation path yet
labels.name: research

title: feat: programmable matter model — agents as reconfigurable atoms (catoms)

body:
## Concept

Every topology formation should have a **term of venery** — a biological collective noun that captures its behavioral essence. Just as a *murder* of crows, a *murmuration* of starlings, or a *parliament* of owls names the collective behavior, not just the species.

### Proposed Naming

| Topology | Current Name | Term of Venery | Biological Source |
|----------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Swarm | `SWARM` | **A Plague** | Locusts — undifferentiated, overwhelming |
| Hierarchy | `HIERARCHY` | **A Parliament** | Owls — deliberation through structure |
| Pipeline | `PIPELINE` | **A Procession** | Caterpillars — processionary single-file |
| Mesh | `MESH` | **A Lattice** | Coral polyps — resilient interconnection |
| Ring | `RING` | **A Carousel** | Dolphins — circular coordination |
| Star | `STAR` | **A Court** | Bees — hub/queen coordination |
| Rhizomatic | `RHIZOMATIC` | **A Mycelium** | Fungi — lateral, underground, no center |
| Fission-Fusion | `FISSION_FUSION` | **A Murder** | Crows — roost/split dynamics |
| Stigmergic | `STIGMERGIC` | **A Colony** | Ants — indirect coordination |

### Implementation

- Add `venery_name` attribute to each topology class
- Use venery names in logging, dashboards, and the DSL
- The YAML agent spec (`AgentForge`) should accept venery names

### Why

The naming system makes the topologies memorable, communicable, and grounded in the biological research that inspired them. "Deploy a murder of agents" is more precise and more evocative than "use fission-fusion topology."

labels.description: Exploratory research — no implementation path yet
labels.name: research

title: feat: terms of venery — biological collective nouns as topology naming system

body:
## The Vacuum

Three missing Next.js infrastructure pieces:

### 1. No `loading.tsx` files
No route has a loading state. When the WebGL shader is compiling or pages are server-rendering, users see a white flash or nothing. Next.js App Router uses `loading.tsx` for Suspense boundaries.

### 2. No custom `not-found.tsx`
404s show the default Next.js page — breaks the visual design.

### 3. No `middleware.ts`
No route protection. Anyone can hit `/admin` without authentication. The admin page checks auth client-side, but the route itself is unprotected at the edge.

## Plan

1. Create `src/app/loading.tsx` — minimal spinner with the cosmic theme
2. Create `src/app/not-found.tsx` — styled 404 matching the glassmorphism design
3. Create `src/middleware.ts` — protect `/admin`, `/settings/schedules`, `/teams` routes; redirect unauthenticated users to sign-in

## Priority

**P1** — loading states affect perceived performance; middleware is a security gap.
title: P1: No loading states, no not-found page, no middleware

body:
## The Vacuum

`src/lib/url-validator.ts` has three bypass vectors:

1. **IPv6 loopback**: `::1` passes because `new URL("https://[::1]").hostname` returns `[::1]` (with brackets), not `::1`. The equality check fails.
2. **file:// scheme**: Input `file:///etc/passwd` gets prepended with `https://` (because it doesn't start with `http`), becoming `https://file:///etc/passwd` which parses as hostname `file` — a valid external domain.
3. **ftp:// scheme**: Same prepend issue — `ftp://evil.com` becomes `https://ftp://evil.com`.

## Plan

1. Strip whitespace and lowercase the input before any processing
2. Check for non-http schemes BEFORE prepending `https://`:
   ```typescript
   if (input.match(/^[a-z]+:\/\//i) && !input.match(/^https?:\/\//i)) {
     throw new Error('Only HTTP(S) URLs are allowed');
   }
   ```
3. For IPv6: strip brackets from hostname before checking:
   ```typescript
   const bare = hostname.replace(/^\[|\]$/g, '');
   if (bare === '::1' || bare === '0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1') throw ...
   ```
4. Block `0.0.0.0` explicitly (already done)
5. Add tests for all three bypass vectors

## Priority

**P1** — SSRF is OWASP Top 10. The scraper, vision, and pagespeed services all use this validator.
title: P1: SSRF validator incomplete — IPv6, file://, ftp:// bypass

body:
## The Vacuum

`db.ts` currently uses an in-memory `Map` as the fallback when Supabase env vars aren't set. On Vercel, this means **all data is lost on every cold start** — audits, teams, leads, subscriptions, everything.

`better-sqlite3` was removed because it crashed Vercel's SSR runtime. The in-memory fallback was a stopgap, not a solution.

## Research

The Neon MCP server is available in the workspace (`mcp__Neon__*`). Neon provides:
- Serverless Postgres (works on Vercel without native modules)
- Branching for dev/staging
- Connection pooling
- Free tier: 0.5GB storage, 190 compute hours/month

Alternatively, Supabase env vars could be configured on Vercel (the `db.ts` Supabase path already works).

## Plan

1. Create a Neon project via MCP or dashboard
2. Run the table creation SQL from `db.ts`'s `TABLE_SCHEMAS` constant (or the equivalent from the old `db-sqlite.ts`)
3. Add `@neondatabase/serverless` to dependencies
4. Replace the in-memory Map fallback in `db.ts` with Neon's serverless driver
5. Set `DATABASE_URL` on Vercel
6. Data persists across cold starts

## Priority

**P0** — without this, the product has no memory. Every Vercel cold start erases all user data.
title: P0: Persistent database — Neon Postgres to replace in-memory Map

## Paper Details

**Title:** The Architecture of Compositional Formal Meaning: A Categorical Framework for Structural Unification of Grammar, Semantics, and Computation
**Author:** A. Padavano (Independent researcher)
**Primary category:** cs.LO (Logic in Computer Science)
**Cross-list categories:** cs.CL, cs.PL, math.CT
**MSC 2020:** 18C50, 03B65, 68Q55, 03G30
**License:** CC BY 4.0

## Abstract (excerpt)

This paper synthesises two prior investigations: one identifying eight structural bridges connecting three traditions of formal semantics (logical, computational, and natural language), and another establishing a fourfold correspondence among grammars, types, proofs, and categories across the Chomsky hierarchy. We ask whether these results unify into a single categorical architecture of compositional formal meaning...

## File Location

`intake/research-adventures-2026-03/arxiv/SYN-01-grand-unified-semantics.tex`

- [x] Paper written and TRP-reviewed
- [x] LaTeX compiled successfully

## Paper Details

**Title:** The Governance Impossibility Thesis: Formal Limits of Self-Describing, Self-Measuring, Self-Organizing Systems
**Author:** A. Padavano (Independent researcher)
**Primary category:** cs.AI (Artificial Intelligence)
**Cross-list categories:** cs.CY, cs.LO, econ.TH
**MSC 2020:** 91B14, 03B25, 68T01, 91B06
**License:** CC BY 4.0

## Abstract (excerpt)

Self-governing systems -- organizations, platforms, software architectures, and AI-augmented institutions that attempt to describe their own structure, measure their own performance, and organize their own coordination -- confront a convergence of impossibility results from three independent traditions. From mathematical logic: Goedel's incompleteness theorems, Tarski's undefinability, and Rice's theorem establish that no sufficiently expressive system can completely describe or decide its own semantic properties...

## File Location

`intake/research-adventures-2026-03/arxiv/SYN-02-governance-impossibility.tex`

## Paper Details

**Title:** The Fourfold Correspondence: Grammar, Automaton, Type, and Proof from Chomsky to Curry-Howard-Lambek
**Author:** A. Padavano (Independent researcher)
**Primary category:** cs.FL (Formal Languages and Automata Theory)
**Cross-list categories:** cs.LO, cs.CL, math.CT
**MSC 2020:** 68Q45, 03B40, 03F03, 18C50
**License:** CC BY 4.0

## Abstract (excerpt)

This paper establishes a fourfold structural correspondence linking formal grammars, abstract automata, type systems, and proof calculi. The classical result in formal language theory pairs each level of the Chomsky hierarchy with a class of abstract machines. We extend this twofold correspondence to a fourfold one by demonstrating that each level admits a canonical type-theoretic characterisation and a corresponding proof-theoretic regime...

## File Location

## Task

Create 8 files in `praxis-perpetua/strategy/defense-rubrics/`:
- `foundations.yaml` (ORGAN-I), `creative-practice.yaml` (ORGAN-II), `applied-systems.yaml` (ORGAN-III), `governance.yaml` (ORGAN-IV), `discourse.yaml` (ORGAN-V), `community.yaml` (ORGAN-VI), `distribution.yaml` (ORGAN-VII), `meta-cognition.yaml` (META)

Each defines faculty-specific dimensions, guest examiner rubric additions, and methodological tradition expectations. Weights are relative (normalized at evaluation time).

body:
## Context

SGO Phase 1 complete (2026-03-20): governance YAMLs, defense rubrics, 4 senate personas, inquiry log all in `praxis-perpetua/`. Phase 2 is next.

## Task

Extract the IRA evaluation machinery from `organvm-i-theoria/auto-revision-epistemic-engine` into a callable interface that `praxis-perpetua` defenses can invoke:

1. Map the callable surface in auto-revision-epistemic-engine (panel convocation, ICC computation, persona loading)
2. Create an adapter that loads SGO-specific personas from `praxis-perpetua/strategy/personas/`
3. Wire defense-protocol.yaml stages into executable steps
4. Produce a certification YAML record in `praxis-perpetua/defenses/records/`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] IRA panel can be convened from praxis-perpetua with SGO personas
- [ ] ICC computation works with faculty-specific + universal rubric dimensions
- [ ] Weight normalization applied per `universal.yaml` (`weight_normalization: true`)
- [ ] Certification record produced in machine-readable YAML
- [ ] At least 1 test defense run (Doc A as Paper tier)

## Dependencies

- `praxis-perpetua/governance/defense-protocol.yaml` (8 stages)
- `praxis-perpetua/strategy/personas/` (4 standing senate personas)
- `praxis-perpetua/strategy/defense-rubrics/universal.yaml` (8 dimensions)
- `organvm-i-theoria/auto-revision-epistemic-engine` (IRA machinery)

Estimated: ~12h | Enables: Phase 3 (first defense)
title: SGO Phase 2: Extract IRA authority module for praxis-perpetua defenses

## Context

The AMP Lab audit (2026-03-19) identified a **critical name collision**: "Amp Lab" is dominated in search by a 7M-subscriber gaming collective and UC Berkeley AMPLab. The channel (@AmpLabMedia, 339 subs) is invisible in every search context.

## Action Required

Discuss with Chris and decide on a rebrand. Options:

1. **"Object Lessons"** — series name becomes channel name. Low competition in film-essay search context.
2. **A compound name** incorporating "object" + "cinema"/"film"
3. **A distinctive coined term** that becomes ownable

## Why This Blocks Everything

- No SEO optimization matters if the channel name drowns in unrelated results
- Kerygma profile and distribution strategy depend on the final brand name
- Social handles need to be claimed under the new name

## Decision Deadline

Before any new content is published. Phase 0, Week 1 of V2 relaunch plan.

**Reference:** `content-pipeline/audits/2026-03-19-amp-lab-audit.md` | `content-pipeline/amp-lab/v2-relaunch-plan.md` (GATE-01)

## ORCID Integration

ORCID (Open Researcher and Contributor ID) provides persistent identity for the human principal — completing the identity chain from system entities (ULIDs) to the creator.

### Integration Points

1. **SPEC document metadata** — add Principal Author ORCID to canonical doc headers
2. **bibliography.bib** — add ORCID to author fields for ORGANVM-authored entries
3. **Source library** — use ORCID API to link author to cited/published works
4. **HUMAN-ACTIONS.md** — link principal identity to ORCID
5. **Preprint submissions** — required for arXiv, Zenodo, most publishers
6. **Grant applications** — increasingly required by NEH, NSF, NEA

### Why

- 3 grounding narratives (20,800 words) structured as publishable papers
- 61 BibTeX entries with DOIs that integrate with ORCID via CrossRef
- ORGANVM's ontology (SPEC-001/002) is built on persistent identity — the creator should have the same guarantee
- Connects to APIs: Semantic Scholar, OpenAlex, CrossRef all support ORCID lookup

### Requires Human Action

Register at orcid.org (free, 2 min) and provide the ORCID iD.
title: Incorporate ORCID persistent identifier into constitutional corpus

# Layer 1: Metaphysical Identity

**Methodology:** RFIV (Research → Formalize → Implement → Verify) with purist rigor
**Plan:** `post-flood/.claude/plans/2026-03-17-spec-implementation-methodology-v3.md`
**Phase State:** `post-flood/specs/PHASE-STATE.md`

## Specs in this Layer

| Spec | Title | Phase | Status |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| SPEC-000 | System Manifesto | R (Research) | **IN PROGRESS** |
| SPEC-001 | Ontology Charter | R (Research) | PENDING |
| SPEC-002 | Primitive Register | R (Research) | PENDING |

## Purpose

Establish what ORGANVM *is* — its identity, ontological commitments, and irreducible primitives. This layer produces no engine code. These documents ARE the constitutional law.

## Academic Traditions

- **Autopoiesis** (Maturana & Varela, Luhmann)
- **Cybernetics** (Ashby, Beer / VSM)
- **Enactivism** (Varela/Thompson/Rosch, Rosen)
- **Institutional Design** (Ostrom / IAD)
- **Social Systems** (Luhmann)

## Deliverables per Spec

- [ ] Literature matrix
- [ ] Theoretical risk register (GROUNDED/ADAPTED/NOVEL/CONTESTED)
- [ ] Grounding narrative (SPEC-000 as paper draft)
- [ ] Current State Inventory
- [ ] Canonical SPEC document
- [ ] Adversarial review (different agent/model)
- [ ] Human spot-check (3+ citations verified)
- [ ] Creator sign-off

## SPEC-000 is the Litmus Test

If the autopoiesis grounding holds, the methodology proceeds. If it reveals naive usage, the Theoretical Correction Protocol corrects the formulation before SPEC-001/002 proceed.
title: SPEC Implementation: Layer 1 — Metaphysical Identity (SPEC-000, 001, 002)

## Severity: Medium

## Problem

Several states have enacted Daily Fantasy Sports statutes that define "fantasy contest" with specific criteria. The whitepaper mentions them in passing (§9.4) but doesn't map Styx's mechanics against each statute's definition.

## Affected Files

- `docs/legal/legal--skill-based-contest-whitepaper.md`
- `docs/legal/legal--50-state-skill-contest-survey.md` (if created per #issue-3)

## Required Analysis (per DFS state)

- Does the statute's definition of "fantasy contest" or "skill-based contest" capture Styx?
- Does Styx need to register/license under the DFS framework?
- Are there entry fee caps, surety bond requirements, or consumer protection mandates?
- Key states: NY (§§ 1400-1410), IN (§ 4-33-24), CO (§ 12-15.5-101), VA (§ 59.1-556), MA (940 CMR 34.00), TN (§ 47-18-5601)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Analysis for ≥10 DFS-legislated states
- [ ] Each state has: statutory citation, definitional analysis, registration requirement, Styx applicability determination
- [ ] Whitepaper §9.4 updated from placeholder to substantive analysis
- [ ] `[COUNSEL: VERIFY]` flags removed for reviewed states
title: Map Styx mechanics against DFS regulatory frameworks by state

## Severity: Medium

## Problem

Consent matrix flags heart rate data as "MAY qualify" as biometric under BIPA (740 Ill. Comp. Stat. 14/15) without analyzing the statutory definition or controlling case law.

## Affected Files

- `docs/legal/legal--cross-jurisdictional-consent-matrix.md`

## Required Analysis

- BIPA § 10 definition of "biometric identifier" — does passively collected wearable heart rate data meet the threshold?
- *Rosenbach v. Six Flags Entertainment Corp.*, 2019 IL 123186 (standing under BIPA)
- *Cothron v. White Castle System, Inc.*, 2023 IL 128004 (per-scan accrual of BIPA claims)
- *Rivera v. Google LLC* (N.D. Ill. 2020) (what constitutes a "biometric identifier")
- Texas CUBI (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 503.001) — parallel analysis
- Washington My Health My Data Act — scope determination for wearable health data

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] "MAY qualify" replaced with definitive analysis + recommendation
- [ ] Illinois consent template updated if heart rate IS biometric
- [ ] Texas CUBI consent template added if needed
- [ ] ≥3 BIPA cases cited with Bluebook format
title: Resolve BIPA biometric data classification for wearable data

## Severity: High

## Problem

§9 of the whitepaper lists "representative jurisdictions" without actual state-by-state statutory analysis. A proper survey is a standalone 30-50 page deliverable.

## Affected Files

- New file: `docs/legal/legal--50-state-skill-contest-survey.md`

## Required Structure (per state)

- Gambling statute citation
- Legal test applied (dominant factor / material element / any chance)
- DFS-specific legislation (if any)
- Skill-contest carve-outs or licensing requirements
- AG opinions or enforcement history relevant to commitment devices
- Risk classification for Styx (Low / Medium / High / Block)
- `STYX_STATE_BLOCKLIST` recommendation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 50 states + DC covered
- [ ] Each entry has statutory citation with Bluebook format
- [ ] Blocklist recommendations backed by specific statutory language
- [ ] Cross-referenced from whitepaper §9 and gatekeeper-compliance §4
- [ ] Minimum 1,500 lines
title: Produce 50-state skill-contest statutory survey

body:
## Severity: High

## Problem

Gold-standard briefs affirmatively address the strongest arguments *against* the position. The whitepaper currently does not seriously engage with counterarguments.

## Affected Files

- `docs/legal/legal--skill-based-contest-whitepaper.md`

## Required Work

Add a new section (between current §9 and §10) titled **"Adverse Authority and Counterarguments"** covering:

1. **Pool-prize argument:** Forfeiture redistribution creates a "pool prize" potentially indistinguishable from gambling pot mechanics
2. **Metabolic variance as chance:** Weight fluctuation from water retention, hormonal cycles, illness could be characterized as a chance element under the material element test
3. **Consideration not eliminated:** The deposit-contract theory hasn't been tested — a court could hold that depositing funds into an escrow that may be forfeited IS gambling consideration
4. **State AG enforcement discretion:** AGs in consumer-protection-aggressive states (NY, CA, IL) could challenge the classification regardless of legal merit
5. **"House always wins" optics:** Platform fee structure means Styx profits regardless of user outcomes — regulators may view this as a house edge

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] New section ≥500 words
- [ ] Each counterargument has a rebuttal with supporting authority
- [ ] Uses *But see* and *Cf.* Bluebook signals appropriately
- [ ] Includes at least 3 adverse cases or AG opinions

labels.description: Needs active follow-up in upcoming operating reviews
labels.name: follow-up

title: Add adverse authority analysis section to whitepaper

body:
## Severity: High

## Problem

Currently 3 cases cited repeatedly (*Murphy v. NCAA*, *White v. Cuomo*, *Dew-Becker v. Wu*). Gold-standard legal analysis requires 15-25 cases including diverse authority types.

## Affected Files

- `docs/legal/legal--skill-based-contest-whitepaper.md`
- `docs/legal/legal--aegis-protocol.md`
- `docs/legal/legal--real-money-activation-brief.md`

## Required Research

- State-level AG opinions on skill-based contest platforms
- Unpublished orders / administrative rulings from state gaming commissions
- DFS-specific litigation (*FanDuel v. Attorney General*, *Interactive Games v. Commonwealth*)
- Commitment device / contingency management case law
- *Humphrey v. Viacom* (DFS class action), *People v. World Interactive Gaming* (NY)
- FinCEN administrative rulings on escrow-as-a-service

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Whitepaper Table of Authorities has ≥15 cases
- [ ] Each new case has proper Bluebook citation with pinpoint page
- [ ] Cases are web-verified via Google Scholar (not overruled/superseded)
- [ ] Supporting docs updated where new cases are relevant

## Summary
Implement the backend side of the Phase 1 beta waitlist funnel.

## Scope
- accept waitlist signups from the landing page and beta application flow
- capture qualification fields and source tags
- send the confirmation email or queue-confirmation response
- preserve source attribution for organic, creator, practitioner, referral, and direct traffic
- expose export/admin access needed for follow-up and cohort admissions

- source attribution is captured with each signup
- confirmation email or equivalent confirmation flow works

body:
**Phase:** Omega (November 2026)

The B2B orchestration panel is the admin interface in the Tauri desktop app for managing enterprise accounts.

Features:
1. View/manage B2B subscriptions and billing status
2. Configure organization-level scopes (which features each org can access)
3. Consumption billing dashboard (API calls, storage, active users per org)
4. Provisioning controls (create sandbox environments, manage API keys)

Complements the web-based HR dashboard (F-WEB-03) — this is the internal operations view while HR dashboard is the customer-facing view. Referenced in enterprise readiness checklist (#298).
title: feat: B2B orchestration panel — billing & scope management (F-DESKTOP-05)

body:
**Phase:** Beta (March 2026)

The Hash Collider is an admin/debug tool in the Tauri desktop app that lets operators test the integrity of our truth log (hash-chained audit trail). It attempts to find collisions or inconsistencies in the chain — if it finds one, something has been tampered with.

The desktop panel UI exists but isn't wired to the backend verification endpoints. This task connects the existing UI to `verifyChain` and displays results (chain intact vs. break detected at block N).

Partially implemented.
title: feat: Hash collider desktop tool wiring (F-DESKTOP-04)

body:
**Phase:** Omega (November 2026)

The HR dashboard is the web interface enterprise customers use to manage their organization's Styx deployment.

Features:
1. Role-based access (admin, manager, viewer)
2. Organization-level settings (allowed contract types, stake limits, reporting preferences)
3. Aggregate analytics (completion rates, engagement trends) without exposing individual user data
4. User management (invite/remove employees, assign coaches)

This is a prerequisite for enterprise sales — coaches and HR managers need a professional dashboard to justify the subscription. Referenced in enterprise readiness checklist (#298).
title: feat: HR dashboard — role-based access & org authorization (F-WEB-03)

body:
**Phase:** Beta (April 2026)

Different US states have different rules about skill-based contests and money handling. This builds the admin UI for toggling which features are available in which states.

For example:
- Some states may require refund-only mode
- Others may prohibit certain stake amounts
- A few may require additional disclosures

The backend geofencing logic exists (TKT-P0-004 / #278), but this is the admin-facing control panel where compliance settings are managed per jurisdiction.

Blocked on H:LC for the state-by-state legal analysis that determines the toggle values.
title: feat: State-by-state compliance toggle UI (F-LEGAL-03)

body:
**Phase:** Gamma (June 2026)

Research shows people are more likely to complete a goal when the final stretch has higher stakes — the "finish line effect."

This feature lets users opt into doubling their stake for the last 20% of their contract. If they've committed $30 for a 30-day No-Contact challenge, they can choose to risk an additional $30 for the final 6 days.

The higher stakes during the most tempting period (when people think "I've already mostly done it, one text won't hurt") dramatically increases completion rates.
title: feat: Finish line spike — double-stake incentive (F-CORE-09)

body:
**Phase:** Delta (September 2026)

The "Ostrich Effect" is a behavioral economics concept: people avoid checking their progress when they suspect bad news.

In Styx, this looks like a user who stops opening the app during difficult stretches — exactly when they need accountability most. This feature detects these avoidance patterns (declining login frequency, skipped proof windows, ignored notifications) and triggers supportive interventions before the user fully disengages.

Connected to churn signal detection in the Customer Success department tracker (#291).
title: feat: Ostrich effect detection (F-AEGIS-09)

body:
**Phase:** Delta (August 2026)

RAIN stands for Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Non-identify — a mindfulness technique for handling difficult emotions.

When our system detects patterns suggesting a user is struggling (missed proofs, late-night app usage, rapid contract changes), it sends a gentle notification using the RAIN framework instead of a generic reminder. This turns Styx from a punitive accountability tool into a supportive one.

**Example:** instead of "You missed your proof!" → "We noticed you might be having a tough day. Here's a moment to pause: Recognize what you're feeling..."
title: feat: RAIN mindfulness notifications (F-AEGIS-08)

body:
**Phase:** Beta (March 2026)

Recovery protocol guardrails are safety limits that prevent users in active recovery (like No-Contact after a breakup) from making impulsive decisions that hurt their progress.

This includes:
- Cooldown periods before changing contract terms
- Warnings when modifying commitments during emotionally vulnerable windows
- Rate limits on contract modifications

Think of it as the "are you sure?" system — but informed by behavioral science about when people are most likely to self-sabotage.

Partially implemented; needs completion and test coverage.
title: feat: Recovery protocol guardrails (F-AEGIS-04)

body: Enterprise customers require formal service guarantees before signing contracts. We need: (1) SLA template with uptime commitment (99.9%), response time guarantees, and remedy clauses, (2) security posture document describing our architecture (encryption at rest/transit, access controls, audit logging), (3) pre-filled security questionnaire template (50-100 common questions with our answers), (4) data processing agreement (DPA) template for GDPR/CCPA compliance. These documents are prerequisites for any B2B sale to a clinic or corporate wellness program. Lives in `docs/enterprise/`.
title: docs: SLA template & enterprise security posture

body: We need the actual marketing execution plan: (1) content calendar with specific topics for 4 posts/month starting July 2026, (2) channel strategy (which platforms, posting frequency, engagement tactics), (3) PR plan for App Store launch (target journalists, press kit, media list), (4) partnership playbook for therapist/coach outreach, (5) referral program mechanics (invite codes, endowed credits, viral loops). This is the operational companion to the GTM strategy. Lives in `docs/marketing/`.
title: docs: Marketing strategy, content calendar & PR plan

body: We need a framework for running experiments on Styx: (1) how to define a hypothesis, (2) how to set up an A/B test (sample size, duration, metrics), (3) how to analyze results (statistical significance, practical significance), (4) user research protocol (interview guides, survey templates, consent forms). Our first planned test is onboarding identity oath variants (Delta phase). This ensures we make product decisions based on data, not gut feeling. Lives in `docs/planning/`.
title: docs: A/B testing framework & user research protocol

body: We need an operational runbook covering: (1) severity classification (SEV-1: money at risk, SEV-2: app down, SEV-3: degraded, SEV-4: cosmetic), (2) escalation procedures (who to call, when), (3) rollback procedures (how to revert a bad deploy), (4) communication templates (what to tell users during an outage), (5) post-mortem process (how we learn from incidents). Monitoring playbook covers: what each alert means, how to acknowledge it, and when to wake someone up. Lives in `docs/operations/`.
title: docs: Incident response runbook & monitoring playbook

body: We need a financial tracking document covering: (1) key KPIs defined with formulas (MRR, churn rate, CAC, LTV, ARPU), (2) monthly/quarterly revenue targets across conservative/moderate/aggressive scenarios, (3) unit economics breakdown for a $39 contract ($9 fee, processing costs, infrastructure costs, net margin), (4) break-even analysis (how many contracts/month to cover costs). This document will be the basis for investor conversations and strategic decisions. Lives in `docs/finance/`.
title: docs: KPI framework, MRR targets & unit economics dashboard

body: We need a formal Go-To-Market strategy documenting: (1) target audience segments for Styx (No-Contact recovery seekers, behavioral change enthusiasts, accountability seekers), (2) channels for reaching them (Reddit communities, therapist referrals, SEO, social media), (3) messaging and positioning (how we explain Styx in one sentence), (4) competitive differentiation (why Styx vs. Beeminder, stickK, DietBet), (5) launch sequence (soft launch → beta → public). This is the foundation for all marketing work. Lives in `docs/marketing/`.
title: docs: GTM strategy & user acquisition plan

body:
## Team Building Timeline (Three Scenarios)

Our hiring plan scales with our ambition and funding. All scenarios share two immediate, non-negotiable hires.

### Non-Negotiable (All Scenarios — Needed NOW)

- [ ] **Mobile Native Engineer (Swift) — Contract** (March 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Build the iOS camera module that captures photo/video proof. This is a physical device feature that AI cannot build — it requires Xcode, a real iPhone, and Swift expertise.
  - **Why urgent:** The camera module is the #1 Beta blocker. Without it, users can't submit proof, and without proof, there's no product. Issues #123, #134.
  - **How to find:** iOS freelancer platforms (Toptal, Gun.io), Swift developer communities, contract through an agency. 2-3 month engagement.

- [ ] **Legal Counsel — Retainer** (March 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Review our custody model (how we hold user money), confirm which states we can operate in, review App Store submission materials, and eventually sign off on the skill-contest whitepaper.
  - **Why urgent:** Multiple Beta-phase tasks require legal sign-off. Issues #132, #133, #136, #146.
  - **How to find:** Fintech/gaming lawyers. Recommendations from founder networks, legal marketplaces.

### Conservative Scenario (Founders + 1 Contractor)
Minimal hiring. Founders handle support, growth, and business development themselves.

- [ ] **Kotlin contractor — short engagement** (May 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Build the Android version of the health data bridge (Google Health Connect). Short engagement, 1-2 months.
  - **Why:** Android parity for Gamma phase. iOS-only is fine for beta but not for public launch.

### Moderate Scenario (Founders + 2-3 Hires)

- [ ] **Mobile Native (Kotlin) — contract or part-time** (May 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Full Android parity — camera module, health bridge, push notifications.
- [ ] **QA / Beta Coordinator — part-time** (May 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Manage the TestFlight beta cohort, triage user feedback, organize bug reports, coordinate testing sessions. This person is the bridge between testers and engineers.
- [ ] **Growth / Community Manager** (September 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Build and manage our online community (Reddit, Discord), create content, manage partnerships with therapists/coaches. This is the person who turns users into advocates.

### Aggressive Scenario (Founders + 4-6 Hires, Assumes Fundraise)

- [ ] **Mobile Native (Kotlin) — full-time** (May 2026)
- [ ] **QA / Beta Coordinator — full-time** (May 2026)
- [ ] **Growth / Community Manager** (July 2026) — Earlier start for community building
- [ ] **Enterprise Sales / BD** (September 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Actively reach out to therapists and coaches, run pilots, negotiate contracts. This role drives B2B revenue.
- [ ] **DevOps / SRE — part-time** (October 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** Production reliability, monitoring, auto-scaling, cost optimization. This role keeps the system running as we grow.
- [ ] **Content / Marketing** (October 2026)
  - **What they'd do:** SEO content, social media, PR for App Store launch. Full-time content creation.
title: hiring: Team building timeline (3 scenarios)

body:
## Enterprise Sales Readiness Gate (November 2026)

Every item must pass before we actively sell to enterprise customers (clinics, coaching agencies, corporate wellness programs). Enterprise customers have higher expectations for security, reliability, and documentation.

- [ ] **SOC 2 Type I audit initiated** — We've engaged an auditor and the process is underway (or we have a documented roadmap with timeline). Enterprise customers ask "are you SOC 2 compliant?" — we need an answer. **How to verify:** Confirm engagement letter with audit firm, or published roadmap. **Owner:** H:LC + H:FO.
- [ ] **Security questionnaire template ready** — Pre-filled answers to the 50-100 most common enterprise security questions (data residency, encryption, access control, incident response). **How to verify:** Review template with 3+ common security questionnaires. **Owner:** Agent + H:LC.
- [ ] **SLA document drafted** — Service Level Agreement defining our uptime guarantee (e.g., 99.9%), support response times, and remedies if we fail. **How to verify:** Legal review completed. **Owner:** Agent + H:LC.
- [ ] **DPA template ready** — Data Processing Agreement for GDPR/CCPA compliance. Enterprise customers need this before signing. **How to verify:** Legal review completed. **Owner:** Agent + H:LC.
- [ ] **Enterprise demo environment live** — A sandboxed Styx instance on Render where prospects can explore without affecting production data. **How to verify:** Visit demo URL, create test contract, verify isolation. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **HR + B2B dashboards functional** — The coach dashboard (F-WEB-03) and orchestration panel (F-DESKTOP-05) are working and presentable. **How to verify:** Walk through demo script with both dashboards. **Owner:** AI.
title: checklist: Enterprise sales readiness gate

body:
## App Store Launch Readiness Gate (October 2026)

Every item must pass before we submit to Apple for App Store review. Apple rejects apps that don't meet their guidelines — failed reviews delay launch by weeks.

- [ ] **App Review submission package ready** — UGC moderation policy, privacy nutrition label, and all required disclosures are written and reviewed by counsel. **How to verify:** Checklist of Apple's App Review Guidelines completed item-by-item. **Owner:** H:LC + H:RO. See Issue #146.
- [ ] **Customer support operational** — A support email/system that Apple's review team can reach. They sometimes email support as part of the review. **How to verify:** Send test email to support address, confirm response within 24 hours. **Owner:** H:FO.
- [ ] **Terms of Service + Privacy Policy approved** — Counsel has reviewed and signed off on our ToS and Privacy Policy. These are linked in the app. **How to verify:** Check for dated counsel approval signature. **Owner:** H:LC.
- [ ] **GDPR data export/deletion tested** — Users can export their data and request deletion. Both flows work in production. **How to verify:** Create a test account, request export, verify the data. Request deletion, verify removal. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **Load testing completed — 1,000 concurrent users** — The system handles App Store-level traffic without degradation. **How to verify:** Run load test, check p99 latency stays under 500ms. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **CDN/WAF production config verified** — Cloudflare CDN caches static content; WAF blocks attack patterns. **How to verify:** Run `scripts/infra/waf-rules.sh`, confirm no misconfigurations. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **Validation gates passing** — `scripts/validation/04-redacted-build-check.sh` and `scripts/gatekeeper-scan.sh` both pass on the release build. **How to verify:** Run both scripts on the production build, confirm green. **Owner:** AI.
title: checklist: App Store launch readiness gate

body:
## Real-Money Pilot Readiness Gate (July 2026)

Every item must be verified before we process real money through the system. Getting this wrong means losing real user dollars or facing legal consequences.

- [ ] **Stripe production keys configured** — We've switched from Stripe test mode to production mode with real banking. **How to verify:** Check Stripe dashboard shows "Live mode" active, process a $1 test charge. **Owner:** H:FO.
- [ ] **High-risk merchant account active** — Our payment processor (Corepay or equivalent) is approved and processing. **How to verify:** Confirm with processor that our account is live. **Owner:** H:BD + H:LC. See Issue #133.
- [ ] **Incident response runbook written** — Step-by-step playbook covering: what to do if payments fail, if data leaks, if the app goes down during an active contract. **How to verify:** Two people can independently walk through the runbook. **Owner:** Agent + H:FO.
- [ ] **Financial reconciliation process documented** — A process to verify our internal ledger matches Stripe's records, run at least weekly. **How to verify:** Run reconciliation, confirm zero discrepancies on test data. **Owner:** Agent + AI.
- [ ] **Legal counsel on-call** — Our lawyer is available for urgent compliance questions during the pilot. **How to verify:** Confirm retainer covers on-call availability. **Owner:** H:FO.
- [ ] **Phantom money check passing in production** — `scripts/validation/01-phantom-money-check.ts` runs against production and finds zero phantom entries. **How to verify:** Run the script, confirm clean. **Owner:** AI.
title: checklist: Real-money pilot readiness gate

body:
## TestFlight Beta Readiness Gate (May 2026)

Every item must be verified before we distribute the app to external beta testers. This protects our reputation and ensures testers have a stable experience.

- [ ] **Sentry error monitoring active** — Our error tracking service is receiving events and alerting us. **How to verify:** Visit Sentry dashboard, confirm events flowing. **Owner:** AI. *Already done.*
- [ ] **Uptime monitoring configured** — A service (UptimeRobot or Better Uptime) pings our API every 60 seconds and pages us if it goes down. **How to verify:** Take down a service intentionally, confirm alert fires. **Owner:** H:RO.
- [ ] **Beta feedback channel live** — A Discord server or dedicated email where testers can report issues. **How to verify:** Send a test message, confirm it arrives. **Owner:** H:FO.
- [ ] **Render plan assessed** — We've evaluated whether our current Render plan handles beta load. Cold starts under 3 seconds. **How to verify:** Run load test, check cold start times. **Owner:** H:RO + H:FO.
- [ ] **Database backup verified** — Backups are running on schedule AND we've tested restoring from one. An untested backup is not a backup. **How to verify:** Restore a backup to a test database, confirm data integrity. **Owner:** AI + H:RO.
- [ ] **Beta readiness smoke test passes** — `scripts/smoke/beta-readiness.sh` passes with `REQUIRE_TARGETS=true`. **How to verify:** Run the script, confirm green. **Owner:** AI.
- [ ] **Apple Developer Account + TestFlight provisioned** — App is uploaded to TestFlight and can be distributed to external testers. **How to verify:** Accept a TestFlight invite on a real device. **Owner:** H:MN + H:RO. See Issue #141.
title: checklist: TestFlight Beta readiness gate

## Launch Operations Timeline (March-December 2026)

Cross-departmental milestones for taking Styx from internal prototype to public product. Each milestone represents a major company event involving multiple departments.

### Milestones

- [ ] **Apple Developer Account + TestFlight setup** (OPS, ENG — H:RO, H:FO — March 2026) — Register for Apple's developer program and configure TestFlight for distributing beta builds to testers. Without this, we cannot ship anything to iPhones.
- [ ] **Hire/contract mobile native engineer (Swift)** (ENG — H:FO — March 2026) — Find and sign a Swift developer to build our iOS camera module. This is the single biggest blocker for everything that follows.
- [ ] **Engage legal counsel (retainer)** (LEG — H:FO — March 2026) — Find a lawyer experienced in fintech/gaming law and sign a retainer. They will review our custody model, state compliance, and whitepaper.
- [ ] **Seed deep agents for LEG, FIN, PRD, OPS** (ALL — AI — March 2026) — Initialize AI agents with domain knowledge so they can start producing draft documents and analyses.
- [ ] **Internal dogfood beta** (ENG, OPS — H:FO — April 2026) — Founders + 5-10 trusted people use the app with test money to find bugs and experience real flows end-to-end.
- [ ] **Monitoring + alerting setup** (OPS — AI + H:RO — April 2026) — Sentry error tracking (done) + uptime monitoring + paging for critical failures.
- [ ] **Support channel setup** (CXS — H:FO — April 2026) — Email + Discord for beta tester feedback and support requests.
- [ ] **TestFlight external beta — 50-100 users** (ENG, OPS, CXS — H:RO, H:FO — May 2026) — First real external users on test-money. Validates core flows with strangers.
- [ ] **Seed user recruitment begins** (GRO — H:FO — May 2026) — Active outreach on Reddit, breakup communities, and through therapist referrals to fill the beta.
- [ ] **Beta feedback synthesis + iteration** (PRD, ENG — AI + H:FO — June 2026) — Analyze beta feedback, prioritize fixes, iterate on the product based on real user behavior.
- [ ] **Real-money pilot — 10-25 users** (FIN, LEG, ENG — H:FO, H:LC — July 2026) — First real dollar through the system. Stakes are held in FBO escrow, settlements are real. Legal must have signed off.
- [ ] **Open beta expansion — 500+ users** (GRO, CXS — H:FO — August 2026) — Wider TestFlight distribution + web access. Support systems must be ready for volume.
- [ ] **App Store submission** (OPS, LEG — H:RO, H:LC — September 2026) — Submit to Apple for review. Package includes UGC moderation policy, privacy nutrition label, and all required disclosures.
- [ ] **App Store launch (public)** (ALL — H:RO — October 2026) — General availability. Anyone can download Styx from the App Store.
title: milestone: Launch operations timeline (Mar-Dec 2026)

## Enterprise Sales / B2B Department — Task Tracker

This tracks our path to selling Styx to therapists, coaches, clinics, and corporate wellness programs. B2B revenue is the long-term growth engine.

### What is B2B2C?
Instead of only selling directly to individuals (B2C), we also sell to professionals (B2B) who then use Styx with their clients (B2B2C). A breakup recovery coach subscribes to Styx at $149/month, then assigns behavioral contracts to their 25 clients. The coach gets a dashboard; the client uses the app.

### Delta Phase (July-September 2026)
- [ ] **Identify 50 target therapists/coaches — ICP definition** (Agent + H:FO, September) — Research and create a list of 50 therapists and coaches who specialize in relationship recovery, breakups, or behavioral change. Define our Ideal Customer Profile: what size practice, what specialization, what price sensitivity.

### Omega Phase (October-December 2026)
- [ ] **Outreach sequence — email + LinkedIn** (Agent + H:BD, October) — Create a 5-email cold outreach sequence and LinkedIn messaging templates for reaching therapists/coaches. Include: introduction, value proposition, case study, demo offer, follow-up.
- [ ] **Enterprise demo environment** (AI, October) — Set up a sandboxed demo version of Styx on Render where prospects can see the coach dashboard, create a test contract, and experience the flow without affecting real data.
- [ ] **Security questionnaire template** (Agent + H:LC, November) — Pre-fill answers to common enterprise security questions: where is data stored, how is it encrypted, who has access, what's our incident response process. Larger clinics and corporate clients will require this.
- [ ] **First pilot onboarding — 1-3 practitioners** (H:BD + H:FO, November) — Get 1-3 therapists or coaches actively using Styx with real clients. This validates the B2B model before we invest in sales.
- [ ] **Enterprise pricing live** (H:FO + H:BD, December) — Publish pricing on the website and enable self-service sign-up for the $49 Starter and $149 Growth tiers. Scale and Enterprise tiers require a sales conversation.
title: Enterprise Sales / B2B Department — Task Tracker

body:
## Skill-Contest Whitepaper Release Gate

**Phase:** Omega (October–November 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-019
**Blocked on:** H:LC (counsel approval with dated signature per version)

### What this does

Before Styx can launch publicly, we need a **legal whitepaper** that proves our contests are skill-based, not gambling. This is a regulatory requirement — jurisdictions that permit skill-based contests require documentation demonstrating the skill element.

This issue builds the **infrastructure** around that whitepaper:

1. **Compliance artifact metadata endpoint** — API for querying the whitepaper's status (current version, signature date, expiration, jurisdictions covered)
2. **`compliance_artifacts` table** — stores whitepaper versions, hashes, counsel signatures, and expiration dates
3. **Legal site section** — a public-facing page displaying the whitepaper with a jurisdiction matrix (which states it covers and their status)
4. **CI release gate** — a check in the deployment pipeline that **blocks deploys** if the whitepaper artifact is missing, expired, or its hash doesn't match the stored version
5. **Hash mismatch rejection tests** — if someone tampers with or accidentally replaces the whitepaper, the system catches it

### Why it matters

This is a **hard launch blocker**. No whitepaper means no public launch. The whitepaper proves to regulators, payment processors, and app store reviewers that Styx operates legal skill-based contests, not illegal gambling. The CI gate ensures we can never accidentally deploy without this critical compliance artifact in place.

### Scope

- Compliance artifact metadata API endpoint
- `compliance_artifacts` table (version, hash, signature, expiration, jurisdictions)
- Legal site section with whitepaper display and jurisdiction matrix
- CI release gate checking artifact presence, expiration, and hash integrity
- Hash mismatch rejection tests
- Artifact versioning (new versions don't delete old ones)
- Counsel signature field (dated, per-version)

### Blockers

The whitepaper **content** itself must be written and approved by legal counsel with a dated signature on each version. This issue covers only the infrastructure — the legal work is a separate human-blocked dependency.

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] API returns current whitepaper status (version, signature date, expiration)
- [ ] `compliance_artifacts` table stores versioned whitepaper metadata
- [ ] Legal site section displays whitepaper and jurisdiction matrix
- [ ] CI pipeline blocks deploys when artifact is missing or expired
- [ ] CI pipeline blocks deploys when artifact hash doesn't match
- [ ] Hash mismatch rejection tests pass
- [ ] Counsel has signed the whitepaper with a dated signature
title: feat: Skill-contest whitepaper release gate (TKT-P1-019)

## Finance & Revenue Department — Task Tracker

This tracks our financial health: how we make money, how we track it, and how we plan for growth.

### Key terms for the non-technical partner:
- **Unit economics**: How much money we make (or lose) per individual transaction
- **MRR**: Monthly Recurring Revenue — predictable income each month
- **CAC/LTV**: Cost to acquire a customer vs. how much that customer pays us over time
- **Reconciliation**: Verifying our internal records match what Stripe (our payment processor) says

### Beta Phase (March-April 2026)

### Gamma Phase (May-June 2026)

### Delta Phase (July-September 2026)

### Omega Phase (October-December 2026)
