body:
## Collusion Slashing & Honey-Trap Enforcement

**Phase:** Gamma (May–June 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-015

### What this does

When the anti-collusion system detects reviewers who are rubber-stamping proofs (approving everything without actually reviewing) or coordinating approvals (colluding with other reviewers), this system enforces consequences:

1. **Slashing** — reducing a reviewer's reputation score. Reviewers with slashed reputation get fewer reviews assigned and earn less. Severe cases result in permanent reviewer ban.

2. **Honey-trap tests** — the system periodically injects obviously fake proofs (e.g., a video of a blank wall submitted as "proof of a 5K run") into a reviewer's queue. If they approve it, they're caught being lazy or dishonest.

3. **Appeals workflow** — because false positives happen, penalized reviewers can appeal. An admin reviews the case and can reverse the penalty.

### Why it matters

A peer-review system is only as good as its reviewers. Without enforcement, lazy or dishonest reviewers degrade the entire system — real accomplishments get cheapened when fake ones slip through. Slashing creates economic incentives for honest reviewing, and honey-traps catch bad actors proactively rather than reactively.

### Scope

- Enforcement evaluation API (trigger slashing based on detection signals)
- `fury_enforcement_cases` table (tracking each enforcement action)
- `fury_penalties` table (slashing amounts, ban durations, appeal status)
- Honey-trap proof generation and injection into reviewer queues
- Appeals workflow (reviewer submits appeal → admin reviews → uphold/reverse)
- Admin enforcement panel (view cases, process appeals, see penalty history)
- Appeal panel for reviewers (submit appeal, track status)
- Legal review of internal reviewer sanctions and appeal rights
- Honey-trap simulation tests (verify detection and penalty application)
- False-positive rollback tests (verify appeal reversal restores reputation)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Detected colluders receive automatic reputation slashing
- [ ] Honey-trap proofs are periodically injected and tracked
- [ ] Reviewers who approve honey-traps are penalized
- [ ] Penalized reviewers can submit appeals
- [ ] Admins can review and resolve appeals
- [ ] Successful appeals fully restore reviewer reputation
- [ ] Legal has reviewed sanctions and appeal rights
- [ ] Tests cover honey-trap simulation and false-positive rollback
title: feat: Collusion slashing & honey-trap enforcement (TKT-P1-015)

body:
## Anti-Collusion Routing — Admin Cluster Screen

**Phase:** Gamma (May 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-008

### What this does

The anti-collusion routing logic — which prevents friends, acquaintances, or coordinating users from reviewing each other's proof — is already built (completed in E2G-20). The system detects social graph connections and routes reviews away from potential collusion pairs.

What's missing is the **admin-facing UI**. Administrators need a screen that shows:

- **Detected collusion clusters** — groups of users who appear to be coordinating (e.g., always approving each other, reviewing in suspicious patterns)
- **Cluster details** — which users are in the cluster, what behavior triggered detection, confidence score
- **Investigation tools** — ability to drill into specific review histories, flag users, or escalate

### Why it matters

Anti-collusion routing prevents the most obvious form of gaming (friends reviewing friends), but sophisticated actors will try to work around it. The admin cluster screen lets human administrators see what the algorithm is detecting and take action on patterns the automated system flags but can't definitively resolve.

### Scope

- Admin screen showing detected collusion clusters
- Cluster visualization (graph or table showing member relationships)
- Drill-down into individual user review histories within a cluster
- Confidence scoring display (how certain the system is about collusion)
- Admin actions: flag for investigation, suspend reviewer privileges, dismiss false positive
- Filtering and sorting (by cluster size, confidence, date detected)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Admins can view all detected collusion clusters
- [ ] Each cluster shows member users, triggering behavior, and confidence score
- [ ] Admins can drill into individual review histories
- [ ] Admin actions (flag, suspend, dismiss) are available and logged
- [ ] Clusters are sortable and filterable
title: feat: Anti-collusion routing — admin cluster screen (TKT-P1-008)

body:
## Fury Audit Masks — Workbench Masked Display

**Phase:** Gamma (May 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-014

### What this does

"Fury" is Styx's peer-review system where anonymous reviewers verify user-submitted proof. The backend for identity redaction — masking names, faces, metadata from submitted media — is already built (completed in E2G-25).

What remains is the **reviewer workbench UI**. This is the screen reviewers use to evaluate submitted proof. It must display:

- **Masked media URLs** — video/image URLs that serve the redacted versions, not the originals
- **Anonymized handles** — reviewers see "User #7A3F", never "John Smith"
- **Stripped metadata** — no EXIF data, no geotags, no timestamps that could identify the submitter

### Why it matters

The entire integrity of peer review depends on anonymity. If a reviewer can identify who submitted the proof, the review is biased — they might approve a friend's weak proof or reject a rival's strong proof. Masked display is the UI enforcement layer that makes the backend redaction meaningful.

### Scope

- Reviewer workbench screen displaying masked media and anonymized handles
- Media URL routing through the redaction layer (never serve raw uploads to reviewers)
- Anonymized handle generation and consistent display
- Metadata stripping verification in the UI layer
- Visual design for the review interface (approve/reject/flag controls)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Reviewers never see original media URLs, names, or identifying metadata
- [ ] Anonymized handles are consistent within a review session
- [ ] Masked media loads correctly in the workbench
- [ ] No path exists for a reviewer to access unmasked content
- [ ] UI provides clear approve/reject/flag controls
title: feat: Fury audit masks — workbench masked display (TKT-P1-014)

body:
## Video Proof Pipeline — Processing State UI

**Phase:** Gamma (May 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-013

### What this does

The video proof pipeline backend is already built (completed in E2G-24). Users can upload video evidence of completing their behavioral contract goals, and the system processes it — hashing for integrity, deduplicating to prevent reuse, and storing it securely. 

What's missing is the **user-facing UI** showing processing states. Right now, after a user uploads a video, they have no visibility into what's happening. They need to see:

- **"Uploading..."** — video is being transferred
- **"Processing..."** — system is hashing, deduplicating, running checks
- **"Verified"** — proof has been accepted and is ready for peer review
- **"Failed"** — something went wrong (with a reason and retry option)

### Why it matters

Users submit video proof as evidence for real-money contracts. If they upload a video and see nothing — no confirmation, no status — they'll assume it failed and submit again (creating duplicates) or contact support. Clear processing state indicators reduce support load and build trust.

### Scope

- Processing state indicators in the mobile app (upload → processing → verified/failed)
- Processing state indicators in the web app
- Real-time state updates (websocket or polling)
- Error states with actionable messages (e.g., "video too large", "format not supported", "duplicate detected")
- Retry flow for failed uploads

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Users see real-time processing state after uploading video proof
- [ ] All four states (uploading, processing, verified, failed) have distinct UI
- [ ] Failed state includes reason and retry option
- [ ] State updates appear within 2 seconds of backend state change
- [ ] Works on both mobile and web
title: feat: Video proof pipeline — processing state UI (TKT-P1-013)

body:
## Health Data Bridge — Native UI Completion

**Phase:** Gamma (May–June 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-007
**Blocked on:** H:MN (hiring mobile native engineer)

### What this does

The server-side API and database for ingesting health data are already built (completed in E2G-23). The system can receive and store data from Apple HealthKit (iOS) and Google Health Connect (Android). What's missing is the **native mobile UI** — the actual screens on iOS and Android where users see their health data feeding into their behavioral contract.

Think of it like this: the plumbing is done, but there's no faucet yet. Users need to see "you walked 8,000 steps today, your contract requires 10,000" in their app.

### Why it matters

Health data integration is a core differentiator for Styx. Behavioral contracts backed by objective health metrics (steps, sleep, heart rate) are more meaningful and harder to game than self-reported progress. But without the native UI, users can't see or interact with this data.

### Scope

- iOS screens (Swift/SwiftUI) showing health data summary tied to active contracts
- Android screens (Kotlin/Compose) showing the same
- Native bridges for reading from HealthKit (iOS) and Google Health Connect (Android)
- Permission request flows for health data access
- Real-time sync indicators (last sync timestamp, sync status)
- Error states (permission denied, no data available, sync failed)

### Blockers

This requires a **mobile native engineer** (Swift for iOS, Kotlin for Android). The native health data bridges (HealthKit/Google Health Connect integration) cannot be done in a cross-platform framework — they require native code. This issue is blocked until that hire is made.

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] iOS users can view health data linked to their contracts
- [ ] Android users can view health data linked to their contracts
- [ ] Permission flows handle grant/deny gracefully
- [ ] Sync status is visible to the user
- [ ] Health data updates reflect in contract progress
title: feat: Health data bridge — native UI completion (TKT-P1-007)

body:
## Self-Exclusion & Responsible-Use Runtime Controls

**Phase:** Beta (March–April 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P1-009

### What this does

Users who recognize they're developing unhealthy patterns with the app should be able to voluntarily lock themselves out for a set period. This is a **self-exclusion** mechanism — common in responsible gaming — where a user says "block me for 30 days" and the system enforces it, no exceptions, no early unlock.

This builds the full self-exclusion flow: the user requests exclusion, picks a duration, and the system blocks them from all contest activity until the period expires.

### Why it matters

Responsible-use controls are both an ethical obligation and a regulatory expectation. Skill-based contest platforms that handle real money must provide users a way to step back if they feel their engagement is becoming compulsive. Several states may require this as a condition of operating.

### Scope

- Self-exclusion API endpoints (create, query status, cannot cancel early)
- `self_exclusions` table tracking user exclusions with start/end timestamps
- Web and mobile compliance page explaining responsible use
- Enforcement guards at all contest entry points (create contract, join contest, submit proof)
- Legal review of updated terms of service and responsible-use page copy
- Tests covering exclusion enforcement at every protected entry point
- Edge case tests: exclusion expires mid-contest, multiple consecutive exclusions, attempted early cancellation

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] User can self-exclude for a chosen duration
- [ ] Self-exclusion cannot be cancelled early by the user
- [ ] All contest entry points reject self-excluded users with a clear message
- [ ] Responsible-use page is accessible from app settings
- [ ] Enforcement tests cover all entry points
- [ ] Legal has reviewed terms and responsible-use copy
title: feat: Self-exclusion & responsible-use runtime controls (TKT-P1-009)

body:
## Forfeit Disposition Policy Engine & Refund-Only Kill Switch

**Phase:** Beta (April 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P0-011
**Depends on:** TKT-P0-001 (settlement), TKT-P0-004 (geofence)
**Blocked on:** H:LC (per-state mode mapping)

### What this does

When a user forfeits a behavioral contract, what happens to their money isn't one-size-fits-all — it depends on which state they're in. Some jurisdictions may require full refunds on forfeit, others allow the platform's standard disposition (e.g., partial retention, redistribution to the contract pool). This builds a **per-jurisdiction policy engine** that looks up the user's state and applies the correct disposition rule.

It also includes an **admin kill switch** that forces the entire platform into refund-only mode. If a legal issue arises (e.g., a state attorney general raises a concern), an admin can flip one switch and every forfeit everywhere becomes a full refund until the issue is resolved.

### Why it matters

Operating a financial product across multiple US jurisdictions means different rules in different states. Applying the wrong forfeit disposition in a state that requires full refunds is a compliance violation. The kill switch provides an emergency brake for legal incidents.

### Scope

- Disposition policy endpoints (lookup by jurisdiction, admin CRUD)
- `jurisdiction_disposition_modes` table mapping each state to its forfeit rules
- Compliance admin panel for viewing and overriding per-state modes
- Refund-only kill switch (single toggle, platform-wide override)
- Counsel sign-off on the per-state mode mapping
- Payout outcome matrix tests (every state × every disposition scenario)
- Override rollback tests (kill switch on → off returns to normal behavior)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Forfeit disposition varies correctly by user jurisdiction
- [ ] Admin can activate refund-only kill switch
- [ ] Kill switch overrides all per-state rules to full refund
- [ ] Kill switch deactivation restores per-state behavior
- [ ] Counsel has signed off on jurisdiction mode mapping
- [ ] Payout matrix tests cover all configured states
title: feat: Forfeit disposition policy engine & refund-only kill switch (TKT-P0-011)

body:
## Geofence Fail-Closed Hardening & Policy Registry

**Phase:** Beta (March–April 2026)
**Ticket:** TKT-P0-004
**Blocked on:** H:LC (state matrix legal review)

### What this does

Currently our geofencing system — which blocks users in states where skill-based contests aren't legally permitted — has a **fail-open** design. If the system can't determine a user's location (GPS unavailable, VPN detected, ambiguous IP), it lets them through. This is a compliance liability.

This issue flips the design to **fail-closed**: if we can't confirm a user is in a legal jurisdiction, they're blocked by default. Only users whose location is positively confirmed as a permitted state get access.

### Why it matters

Fail-open geofencing means a user in a restricted state could slip through during a location lookup failure. In a regulated skill-contest environment, that's a potential legal violation. Fail-closed is the industry standard for compliance-sensitive applications.

### Scope

- Flip geofence logic to fail-closed (block unless confirmed safe)
- Add **policy introspection endpoint** so clients can query which jurisdictions are available
- Create `compliance_decisions` table to log every geofence decision (permit/deny + reason)
- Build **state availability matrix UI** showing which states are open/closed and why
- Counsel sign-off on the state matrix before it goes live
- Route-by-route geofence guard regression tests (every protected endpoint must be tested)

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] No user can access contest features without a confirmed-legal geolocation
- [ ] Policy introspection endpoint returns current state matrix
- [ ] Every geofence decision is logged in `compliance_decisions`
- [ ] State matrix UI is accessible to admins
- [ ] Regression tests cover all protected routes
- [ ] Counsel has signed off on the state availability matrix
title: feat: Geofence fail-closed hardening & policy registry (TKT-P0-004)

body:
## Motivation

The system currently depends on external AI APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq) for LLM inference, embeddings, and agent orchestration. Investigate a concrete path to running the full ORGANVM agentic stack on locally-hosted models — eliminating API cost, latency, and vendor lock-in.

## Current State

**Already in place:**
- `LLMAdapter` ABC in `adapters/base.py` with clean provider abstraction
- `LLMRouter` in `adapters/router.py` with strategy-based routing (`COST_OPTIMIZED` prefers local)
- `OllamaAdapter` — fully implemented (complete, stream, embed)
- `LLMProvider.LOCAL` enum value exists but has **no implementation**
- `local-llm` optional extra installs `ollama>=0.1.0`
- `sentence-transformers` extra for local embeddings (not wired into adapter layer)
- `chromadb` extra for local vector DB
- RLHF pipeline extras (`trl`, `peft`, `accelerate`, `datasets`) for fine-tuning

**Hard-coupled to external APIs:**
- `agent--claude-smith` imports `@anthropic-ai/sdk` directly in orchestrator — by design (Claude Agent SDK wrapper)
- `AnthropicAdapter` and `OpenAIAdapter` are core dependencies in `pyproject.toml`
- Embedding fallback in router prefers OpenAI (`text-embedding-3-small`)
- Anthropic/Groq adapters fall back to `hash_embedding` (lossy) when no embed API available

## Proposed R&D Phases

### Phase 1: Local-first inference (config-only)
- Run agentic-titan with Ollama + quantized models on Apple Silicon (M3/16GB → 7B-13B models)
- Validate all 9 topology patterns work with Ollama backend
- Document quality/speed tradeoffs vs API providers

### Phase 2: Local embeddings + vector store
- Wire `sentence-transformers` into `OllamaAdapter.embed()` (or a shared local embed util)
- Remove OpenAI as default embedding fallback in `LLMRouter.embed()`
- Ensure ChromaDB works as default vector store without external API calls

### Phase 3: Implement `LocalAdapter` (in-process inference)
- Build the `LLMProvider.LOCAL` adapter using llama.cpp / MLX for direct Apple Silicon inference
- Skip Ollama HTTP layer — run models in-process
- Support GGUF model loading, quantization selection

### Phase 4: Agent-layer sovereignty
- Either: (a) make `agent--claude-smith` model-agnostic by routing through titan's adapter layer, or (b) build `agent--local-smith` equivalent using titan router
- Ensure session persistence, self-correction, and subagent spawning work with local models

### Phase 5: Fine-tuning pipeline
- Use existing RLHF extras to fine-tune local models on ORGANVM orchestration traces
- Specialize a small model for the system's specific agent patterns and tool-calling conventions

## Constraints

- 16GB RAM limits practical local models to 7B-13B quantized
- Quality gap is real for complex planning/code-gen — hybrid approach (local for volume, API for hard reasoning) may be the pragmatic steady-state
- `COST_OPTIMIZED` routing strategy already supports this hybrid model

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All agentic-titan topologies pass tests with Ollama-only backend
- [ ] Zero external API calls for embeddings and vector search
- [ ] `LLMProvider.LOCAL` adapter implemented and tested
- [ ] At least one agent workflow runs end-to-end with no external API calls
- [ ] Cost-optimized routing demonstrably prefers local → falls back to API only when needed
title: R&D: Path to full local-inference sovereignty (eliminate external AI API dependencies)

body:
## Context

The four-branch synthesis report (Section VI.4) identified that the ORGANVM system is entirely self-referential. No session addressed how external APIs, third-party services, or open-source dependencies fit into the unidirectional dependency model.

## Problem

Products in Organ III depend on external services (Stripe, Vercel, Neon, Redis, etc.) that are not part of any organ. The governance model has no concept of external inputs beyond the `intake/` directory (which handles content, not services).

## Open Questions

- Are external services honorary Organ I inputs (foundational primitives)?
- Should they be tracked in seed.yaml as `consumes` edges with a special `source: EXTERNAL` designation?
- How do external API changes propagate through the governance model? (e.g., a Stripe API deprecation affecting 5 Organ III products)
- Should there be an "external dependency registry" parallel to registry-v2.json?

## Proposed Deliverables

- [ ] Policy document in `praxis-perpetua/standards/` or `organvm-corpvs-testamentvm/docs/`
- [ ] seed.yaml schema update to support external dependency declarations
- [ ] Audit of current external dependencies across Organ III repos

## Source

- Synthesis report Section VI.4
- All four sessions treated the system as entirely self-contained
title: Define external dependency policy (third-party services in unidirectional model)

## Context

The four-branch synthesis report (Section VI.3) identified that all analyses treat products in isolation. With 28 Organ III repos, no model exists for how they interact with each other.

## Open Questions

- Do multiple Organ III products compete for ORGAN-IV orchestration bandwidth?
- Can products share infrastructure (databases, auth, billing) without violating the single-repo-per-product model?
- How do seed.yaml `produces`/`consumes` edges between Organ III repos work? Are intra-organ dependencies governed differently from cross-organ ones?
- When two products both consume the same Organ I artifact, do they coordinate or diverge?

## Proposed Research

- Audit existing Organ III seed.yaml files for intra-organ edges
- Model resource contention for ORGAN-IV dispatch
- Determine if a shared-services repo pattern is valid within the organ model

## Source

- Synthesis report Section VI.3
- Four sessions all analyzed single products in isolation
title: Model multi-product interference within Organ III

body:
## Context

The four-branch synthesis report (Section IV.D) and the materia-collider founding document both identify a gap in the director/specialist composition model: the director currently must know skill names to invoke specialists. There is no automated mapping from directorial intent to the right archetype(s).

## Problem

With 70+ skills in `a-i--skills/` and 22 archetypes in `agentic-titan`, the director manually selects which specialist to use. This doesn't scale. The director's job is to perceive what's missing and express intent — not to maintain a mental index of available specialists.

## Proposed Solution: "Casting Director" Process

1. Map directorial intent expressions to skill/archetype capabilities
2. Semantic matching: "this project needs protection" -> `security-threat-modeler` + `contract-risk-analyzer`
3. Could live as a collider protocol or as an ORGAN-IV orchestration feature
4. Should compose multiple specialists when intent spans domains

## Open Questions

- Should this be a skill itself, a Titan topology, or a collider protocol?
- How does it interact with the existing `/skill-name` invocation pattern?
- What's the minimum viable version (keyword matching vs. semantic search)?

## Source

- Synthesis report Section IV.D and Section VI.5
- Session 592f6517 (architectural validation)
- `materia-collider/genesis/2026-03-06-synthesis--director-habitat-specialist-collider.md` (Open Thread #3)
title: Design intent-routing layer for director -> specialist matching

body:
## Context

The four-branch synthesis report identified **failure recycling** as an unconsidered gap (Section VI.2). When products are archived (GRADUATED -> ARCHIVED), their theoretical insights, algorithms, and structural patterns should be formally recyclable back into Organ I as raw material.

## Problem

The promotion state machine ends at ARCHIVED. There is no formal process for extracting value from dead products and routing it upstream. Valuable theoretical work embedded in failed products is lost.

## Proposed Solution: The Ossuary Pattern

Named after the architectural practice of storing bones for future use:

1. Define an ARCHIVED -> REABSORBED transition (or a parallel "harvest" process)
2. Create a template for extracting theoretical primitives from archived repos
3. Route extracted material through the Logos Bypass (see #98) into Organ I intake
4. Track provenance: which Organ I work derives from Organ III failures

## Deliverables

- [ ] Document the Ossuary pattern in `praxis-perpetua/standards/` or as a collider protocol
- [ ] Add `reabsorption_status` field consideration for registry-v2 schema
- [ ] Test on one archived repo

## Source

- Synthesis report Section VI.2
- Session 8738e514 (strategic lifecycle analysis)
title: Define Ossuary pattern: ARCHIVED repo reabsorption into Organ I

body:
## Context

The four-branch synthesis report (`materia-collider/experiments/2026-03-07-four-branch-product-implementation-synthesis.md`) identified the **Logos Bypass** as the primary resolution to the echo-chamber problem created by strict unidirectional dependencies (I->II->III).

## Problem

When an Organ III product discovers a flaw in upstream theory (Organ I) or art (Organ II) based on market feedback, governance explicitly forbids structural back-edges. Without a formal feedback mechanism, Theory and Art operate in a vacuum.

## Proposed Solution

Formalize the **Logos Bypass** as an SOP in `praxis-perpetua/standards/`:

1. Organ V (Logos) documents market friction as a public essay or analytical report
2. Organ VI (Koinonia) discusses the friction in community forums
3. These outputs are pushed to `intake/` as neutral staging
4. Organ I autonomously ingests reports as new raw material

The feedback loop closes *semantically* (through discourse) rather than *structurally* (through code), preserving the acyclic dependency graph.

## Deliverables

- [ ] `SOP--logos-bypass-feedback-routing.md` in `praxis-perpetua/standards/`
- [ ] Collider experiment: test the protocol on a real case (e.g., Styx lambda=1.955 hardcoded constant)
- [ ] Document the `intake/` staging conventions for market intelligence vs. untrusted material

## Source

- Synthesis report Section IV.A
- Session 8738e514 (meta-organvm strategic analysis)
- Derived Principle P1: "Structural integrity through semantic flexibility"
title: Formalize Logos Bypass protocol for semantic feedback routing
